serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw green lettuce is approximately 47 g (about 1 cup shredded), commonly used as a salad base or garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Lettuce, green leaf, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169247)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw green leaf lettuce. HPB Singapore values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are consistent with USDA data and are retained as primary values per input provenance. USDA used to supplement saturated fat, potassium, vitamin C, vitamin A, and other micronutrients not reported in HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lettuce is a non-starchy leafy vegetable with negligible digestible carbohydrate. A GI of approximately 15 is widely cited in the University of Sydney GI Database for leafy vegetables including lettuce. GL per serving calculated as: GI (15) × available carbohydrate per serving (47 g × 2.87 g/100 g = 1.35 g) / 100 = 0.20, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (28 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (194 mg/100 g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Raw green lettuce is appropriate for hypertensive individuals; its very low sodium content (28 mg/100 g) and potassium contribution (194 mg/100 g) support the DASH dietary pattern, and its dietary nitrates may contribute to mild vasodilation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.87 g/100 g), low GI (~15), and negligible sugar (0.78 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw green lettuce has a very low glycemic index (~15) and minimal digestible carbohydrate, making it an ideal non-starchy vegetable for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, consistent with ADA and HPB dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and folate (38 mcg DFE/100 g) supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw green lettuce is a heart-healthy food with negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and useful amounts of folate and potassium; regular consumption of leafy greens is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant vitamins (vitamin C 9.2 mg, vitamin A 148 mcg RAE) and calcium (36 mg/100 g) supporting bone and immune health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw green lettuce provides antioxidant vitamins C and A that help combat oxidative stress relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; however, it lacks omega-3 fatty acids and contains less than 3 g dietary fibre per 100 g, so it should be consumed as part of a broader anti-inflammatory dietary pattern rather than as a primary therapeutic food.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw green lettuce (Lactuca sativa) is a fresh, uncooked leafy vegetable belonging to the Asteraceae family, widely consumed globally as a salad base, sandwich filling, wrap, and garnish. It is characterised by its crisp, tender leaves, mild flavour, and high water content (~95%). Nutritionally, it is extremely low in calories (15 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (2.87 g/100 g), with negligible fat (0.15 g) and modest protein (1.36 g). It provides dietary fibre (1.3 g), calcium (36 mg), iron (0.86 mg), potassium (194 mg), folate (38 mcg DFE), vitamin A (148 mcg RAE), and vitamin C (9.2 mg) per 100 g. Its very low glycemic index (~15) and minimal digestible carbohydrate make it ideal for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. The low sodium (28 mg) and useful potassium content support DASH-aligned dietary patterns for hypertension. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is suitable for virtually all dietary patterns including ketogenic, low-calorie, and plant-based diets. Raw green lettuce is used extensively in Western, Asian, and Middle Eastern cuisines and is available year-round in Singapore and globally.
common_user_queries: Can I eat lettuce on a keto diet?
Is lettuce good for high blood pressure?
What vitamins are in green lettuce?
How much sodium is in lettuce?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95, reviewed canonical record). Micronutrients and fatty acid profile supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169247) for raw green leaf lettuce — an exact match. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database for leafy vegetables. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated between HPB and USDA.
notes: trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy for raw lettuce). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food contains no cholesterol). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food contains no B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — negligible in raw lettuce per USDA). No -1 sentinel values require documentation as all fields have been populated from USDA SR Legacy data. The rheumatoid_arthritis suitability from the input record has been corrected: the input incorrectly set suitable=false while providing a positive rationale; the corrected record sets concern_level=low with an honest clinical note acknowledging both the antioxidant benefit and the limitations. Keto dietary tag retained from input as lettuce is compatible with ketogenic diets given its very low net carbohydrate content.
